ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general crab food product class; no specific steamed crab with ginger entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers cooked crabs broadly; no steamed-with-ginger specific code exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for steamed crab dish; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of steamed crab in Singapore is approximately 200g (roughly half a medium-sized crab), consumed as a main dish or shared seafood course.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed crab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database (cr_id F03060034)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists 'Steamed crab' (with ginger) as a named entry with cr_id F03060034. All core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate), sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are sourced directly from this HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (0.3g), monounsaturated fat (0.3g), polyunsaturated fat (0.6g), potassium (~240mg), and cholesterol (~78mg) are supplementary estimates adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked blue crab (NDB No. 15139), as HPB does not report these specific values for this entry; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed per HPB Foodo F03060034
gi_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore — nutrient basis; University of Sydney GI Database consulted and confirms no GI value exists for pure protein-seafood foods with zero carbohydrate
gi_source.source_match_notes: Steamed crab contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g as confirmed by HPB Foodo (F03060034). The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and does not list a GI value for steamed crab or equivalent zero-carbohydrate seafood, consistent with the established principle that GI is not applicable to foods with negligible carbohydrate content. glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable per Veritas critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 461mg/100g exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold relevant to hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit steamed crab portions to approximately 150g per serving and strictly avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or salted dipping sauces, as the baseline sodium load of 461mg/100g already represents a substantial fraction of the 2000mg daily sodium target recommended by Singapore HPB and DASH guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content with high protein (17.3g/100g) eliminates post-prandial glucose spike risk from the crab itself
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed crab is an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, as its zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content (confirmed per HPB Foodo F03060034) produces no glycemic response; however, patients should avoid sweet chili, plum, or other sugar-containing dipping sauces that would add carbohydrate load to the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (461mg/100g) is a cardiovascular risk factor, partially offset by low saturated fat (0.3g/100g), high protein, and beneficial calcium (156mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, steamed crab offers a low-saturated-fat, high-protein seafood option, but the sodium content of 461mg/100g warrants portion control (recommended no more than 150g per serving) and avoidance of salty condiments to remain within cardiovascular-safe sodium limits; the low saturated fat and absence of trans fat are positive attributes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High calcium (156mg/100g) supports bone health in RA patients on corticosteroids; sodium may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Steamed crab is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its high calcium content (156mg/100g) supporting bone density in those on corticosteroid therapy and its very low saturated fat (0.3g/100g); however, the sodium content of 461mg/100g may exacerbate RA-related tissue oedema and patients should avoid additional salty condiments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Crab with Ginger is a classic Singaporean and Chinese seafood dish in which whole or halved fresh crab is steamed over high heat with slices of fresh ginger, which impart a subtle warmth and help neutralise any fishy odour. The dish is a staple of Singaporean seafood restaurants and home cooking, prized for its clean, sweet, and delicate flavour that showcases the natural sweetness of fresh crab meat without the use of heavy sauces or frying. The texture of the crab meat is tender and slightly firm, with the shell turning bright orange-red upon steaming. Nutritionally, steamed crab is a high-protein (17.3g/100g), low-fat (2.0g/100g), and very low-calorie (87 kcal/100g) seafood option with zero carbohydrates and zero sugar, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic-friendly dietary patterns. It is notably rich in calcium (156mg/100g), which is significant for bone health, and provides moderate iron (0.8mg/100g). The primary nutritional caution is its elevated sodium content (461mg/100g), which requires portion awareness for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The steaming preparation method avoids the formation of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) associated with high-heat dry cooking, and the ginger component contributes anti-inflammatory gingerols. This dish is gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-compatible when prepared without non-halal additives.
common_user_queries: How many calories in steamed crab?
Is steamed crab good for diabetics?
Can I eat steamed crab if I have high blood pressure?
How much protein is in steamed crab?
What is the sodium content of steamed crab?

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database under the exact named entry 'Steamed crab' (cr_id F03060034) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Fatty acid breakdown (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated), potassium, and cholesterol are supplementary estimates adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked blue crab (NDB 15139) as HPB does not report these specific values; this adaptation is documented in notes.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (F03060034); steamed crab is a pure protein-and-fat seafood with no carbohydrate-containing ingredients — naturally zero. sugar_g is confirmed zero as a direct consequence of zero carbohydrate — naturally zero. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as crab contains no plant fibre — naturally zero. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as crab contains negligible trans fat, confirmed zero by formulation for this seafood type. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for F03060034 and detailed micronutrient data for this specific Singapore steamed crab preparation is not available in the HPB database as of 2026. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15139, blue crab cooked) was consulted for fatty acid fractions, potassium (~240mg/100g), and cholesterol (~78mg/100g) as supplementary estimates. 3. FATTY ACID ADAPTATION: saturated_fat_g (0.3g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.3g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked blue crab (NDB 15139) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown for this entry. 4. GI NOT APPLICABLE: Steamed crab contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g (confirmed per HPB Foodo F03060034). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. glycemic_index set to null, gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and lists no GI value for zero-carbohydrate seafood, consistent with established GI methodology. 5. OCCASION TAG REVIEW: The input record listed 'breakfast' as an occasion tag; this has been reclassified to dinner/main course as steamed crab is not a typical breakfast food in Singapore or Chinese culinary tradition. 6. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches (closest_parent) as no exact steamed-crab-with-ginger entry exists in either ontology system; gap notes provided.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Steamed Crab with Ginger' is precise and unambiguous. Mandatory Singaporean multilingual identity satisfied: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi all present.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (87.0), protein_g (17.3), fat_g (2.0), carbohydrate_g (0.0) all present and numeric. Sourced from HPB Foodo cr_id F03060034 (approved primary source). nutrient_source block names the exact HPB entry and documents USDA SR Legacy supplementary adaptation for fatty acids and potassium.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (461.0), saturated_fat_g (0.3), sugar_g (0.0) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible and supported by HPB and USDA SR Legacy sources.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is null (not 0) per Veritas critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. gi_category is 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. gi_source documents the HPB carbohydrate confirmation and University of Sydney GI Database consultation. All required GI fields present and correctly set.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI (FOODON_03311737, crab food product) and FoodEx2 code (A0CJW, Crabs cooked) both present as defensible closest-parent matches with gap notes. SNOMED CT null with honest gap note. No invented codes.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium-focused), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, zero-carbohydrate basis), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, sodium vs. low saturated fat). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name fields correctly set.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains all strings from aliases_by_language (self-check passed). Coverage includes alternate English names, Chinese script (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms. Aliases are comprehensive and consistent between aliases and aliases_by_language.
